hero.tagline: Many robots, one outcome - architectures, MAPF, and swarms
hero.intro: Multi-robot systems are not just one robot times N. You inherit new problems: who decides who does what, how robots avoid each other, how they share a map, how they survive a comms blackout, and how the system degrades when one robot fails. This branch covers the canonical patterns: centralized vs decentralized architectures, multi-agent path finding (MAPF), multi-robot SLAM, market-based task allocation, formation control, and the swarm-style algorithms that scale to hundreds of agents. If your fleet is bigger than two, you need this.

sections.heading: Why multi-robot? When the cost is worth it
sections.body: Putting two robots in the same space costs more than twice as much in software complexity. You only do it when the task demands it: parallel coverage (warehouse picks across many aisles), heterogeneous capabilities (a quadruped scouts, a wheeled robot carries), redundancy (if one dies the others continue), or scale (a swarm of inspection drones). The benefit is throughput and resilience; the cost is coordination, communication, and the possibility of cascading failures.
bullets: Parallel coverage - same task, different sub-areas (warehouse picks)
Heterogeneity - different robots, complementary skills (drone scouts + ground vehicle delivers)
Redundancy - N+1 robots so a failure doesn't halt the system
Scale - swarms of inspection / mapping / agricultural robots
Cost: every robot is a comms peer; every coordination decision adds latency
Cascading failure: a stuck robot can deadlock the fleet if traffic logic is poor

sections.heading: Architectures - centralized, decentralized, hierarchical
sections.body: Centralized systems have one brain (the fleet manager) that sees everything and dispatches every task. Decentralized systems push decisions to each robot - they negotiate peer-to-peer using algorithms like consensus, market auctions, or local rules. Hierarchical is the practical middle ground: a central scheduler hands out high-level tasks; robots negotiate locally to resolve conflicts at intersections.
bullets: Centralized - easy to reason about, single point of failure, doesn't scale past ~50 robots
Decentralized - robust to comms loss, hard to debug, no global optimum
Hierarchical - central for slow strategic decisions, local for fast tactical ones
Pure swarm - no central coord; robots follow local rules (boids), emergent behavior
Real systems: Open-RMF and most commercial fleets are hierarchical

codeBlocks.title: Average-consensus update - agree on a value across N robots
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

def consensus_step(x, neighbors, eps=0.15):
    """x: my current value. neighbors: list of neighbour values.
       Returns updated value pulled toward the average."""
    if not neighbors: return x
    return x + eps * (np.mean(neighbors) - x)

# Each robot runs this each tick, reading its neighbours' broadcast values.
# After enough ticks (~50-100 on a connected graph), all robots converge to the
# initial average - an emergent global agreement, no central coordinator needed.

sections.heading: Communication - DDS, MQTT, namespacing, dropouts
sections.body: ROS 2 DDS works fine within one robot but doesn't scale to fleets - every robot would discover every node on every other robot. Production fleets use one of: ROS_DOMAIN_ID per robot to isolate, FastDDS Discovery Server (centralized discovery), or a higher-level transport (MQTT, ZeroMQ, gRPC) bridging only the topics that need cross-robot visibility. Plan for the comms link to drop - Wi-Fi mesh, 5G, LoRa all have failure modes.
bullets: Per-robot ROS_DOMAIN_ID - isolates DDS chatter; bridge cross-robot topics deliberately
FastDDS Discovery Server - single discovery hub; robots register, find each other through it
MQTT / Zenoh - pub/sub across the WAN, lossy-tolerant; bridge to local ROS topics
Out-of-band: cellular fallback when Wi-Fi drops; BLE for proximity
Always design for partition: each robot must keep operating safely without comms
Topic load: don't bridge images cross-fleet; bridge only state + commands

codeBlocks.title: Robot-A and Robot-B isolated by domain
codeBlocks.code:
# On Robot A
export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=10
# On Robot B
export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=20

# On the fleet manager bridge node
export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=10
ros2 run domain_bridge domain_bridge \
    --ros-args -p config_file_path:=/etc/bridge.yaml

codeBlocks.title: Bridge selected topics A → fleet manager
codeBlocks.code:
name: amr-fleet-bridge
topics:
  /amr_a/odom:
    type: nav_msgs/msg/Odometry
    from_domain: 10
    to_domain: 99
    qos:
      durability: volatile
      reliability: reliable
      history: keep_last
      depth: 10
  /amr_a/state:
    type: industrial_msgs/msg/RobotStatus
    from_domain: 10
    to_domain: 99

sections.heading: Multi-agent path finding (MAPF)
sections.body: Single-robot path planning is well solved. Multi-robot is hard: you need paths that don't collide in space-time. The algorithm of choice is CBS (Conflict-Based Search) and its faster variant ECBS. CBS plans each robot independently, detects pairwise conflicts, and adds constraints to resolve them - optimal but exponential in conflicts. ECBS trades a small bound on suboptimality for orders-of-magnitude speedup. PBS is even faster for sparse problems.
bullets: Cooperative A* - replan one agent at a time given others' fixed paths; fast but suboptimal
CBS - optimal, two-level: high-level resolves conflicts, low-level replans single agents
ECBS - bounded-suboptimal CBS; w-times the optimal cost in fraction of the time
PBS - priority-based search; faster on sparse problems, no completeness guarantee
Anytime SIPP - single-agent planner respecting time-windows from other agents
Lifelong MAPF - replan continuously as new tasks arrive (warehouses)

sections.heading: Task allocation - auctions, market-based, optimal
sections.body: When N tasks arrive and M robots are free, who gets what? The optimal answer is the assignment problem (Hungarian algorithm, O(M³) - fine for small fleets). For large fleets, market-based methods scale better: each robot bids on tasks based on its expected cost (distance, congestion, battery), and the auctioneer awards each task to the lowest bidder. CBBA (Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm) extends this to bundles of tasks per robot.
bullets: Hungarian - optimal for assignment; O(M³); breaks down past ~1000 robots/tasks
Greedy - pick the lowest-cost (robot, task) pair; fast, suboptimal, easy to reason about
Auction-based - robots bid; auctioneer awards; communication cost per task
CBBA - robots bid on bundles; consensus on assignments; distributed
MILP - model the whole problem as integer linear programming; optimal but slow
Reinforcement learning - for non-stationary fleets, learn a dispatch policy

codeBlocks.title: Hungarian assignment with scipy
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment

# cost[i][j] = cost for robot i to do task j
cost = np.array([
    [4, 1, 3],
    [2, 0, 5],
    [3, 2, 2]
])
row_ind, col_ind = linear_sum_assignment(cost)
for r, t in zip(row_ind, col_ind):
    print(f'robot {r} → task {t} (cost {cost[r, t]})')
print('total cost:', cost[row_ind, col_ind].sum())

sections.heading: Multi-robot SLAM - building the map together
sections.body: Two robots in the same building can build a map twice as fast - if they can recognize when they've seen the same place and stitch their submaps together. Loop closure across robots is the hard part: appearance descriptors (BoVW, NetVLAD) match candidate places, then geometric verification (RANSAC, ICP) confirms the relative transform. Open-source stacks: LAMP (Lidar-based, NASA JPL), DCL-SLAM (decentralized lidar), Kimera-Multi (visual-inertial).
bullets: Centralized: each robot streams lidar/keyframes to a base station that runs the joint optimization
Decentralized: robots exchange compact place descriptors; only solve when a match is found
Cross-robot loop closure: NetVLAD or HF-Net descriptors + ICP / PnP verification
Pose graph fusion: GTSAM / Ceres / g2o run on the merged graph
Trust: bad cross-robot loop closures destroy the map - use M-estimators or DCS
LAMP, DCL-SLAM, Kimera-Multi, Swarm-SLAM are the modern open-source baselines

sections.heading: Formation control - moving as a team
sections.body: Sometimes you want N robots to maintain a shape - line formation for an inspection sweep, V-formation for fuel efficiency, custom shape for a payload. Three main flavors: leader-follower (one leader, N-1 followers tracking a relative pose), virtual structure (treat the formation as a single rigid body), and behavior-based (each robot follows local rules: maintain offset to neighbors, avoid collisions). Each has tradeoffs in robustness vs precision.
bullets: Leader-follower - simple but the leader is a single point of failure
Virtual structure - formation has its own pose; robots track their slot inside it
Behavior-based - local rules per robot; robust to failures; less precise shape
Consensus protocols - robots agree on a common reference (centroid, heading)
Saturated control - clamp commands so a stragger can catch up without others crashing
Real example: drone shows (Intel, Skymagic) use centralized choreography + GPS RTK

sections.heading: Swarm robotics - local rules, emergent behavior
sections.body: Swarm robotics scales to hundreds of agents by abandoning centralized control. Each robot follows local rules - Reynolds' boids (1986) is the canonical demo: cohesion (steer toward neighbors' centroid), alignment (match average velocity), separation (avoid getting too close). With those three rules, you get flocking, schooling, herding behaviors that look choreographed but emerge from local interactions. The widget below is the original boids algorithm; play with the weights to see how each rule contributes.
bullets: Cohesion - pulls each agent toward its neighbors' centroid (groups stay together)
Alignment - matches each agent's velocity to its neighbors' average (synchronized motion)
Separation - pushes agents apart at close range (collision avoidance)
Emergent: no agent knows the global state; the swarm self-organizes
Real systems: Kilobots (Harvard), Smarticles, drone shows, agricultural drone swarms
Ant colony optimization, particle swarm optimization - algorithms inspired by swarms

sections.heading: Consensus & distributed coordination
sections.body: Decentralized fleets need agreement: which robot takes the next task, who is the leader, what is the agreed map version. Distributed consensus algorithms (Raft, Paxos, gossip protocols) solve this in the face of network partitions and failures. For robotics-specific uses, average consensus is enough - robots iteratively share state with neighbors and converge to the network-wide average.
bullets: Average consensus - x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + ε Σ_j (x_j - x_i); converges if graph is connected
Raft / Paxos - strong consistency; needed when an order matters
Gossip protocols - eventually consistent; cheap; great for large swarms
Byzantine fault tolerance (PBFT) - for adversarial environments; rarely needed in robotics
Leader election: ring algorithm, bully algorithm; usually layered on top of Raft

sections.heading: Real deployments - what production looks like
sections.body: Real multi-robot deployments are a layered cake. At the bottom: per-robot ROS 2 + Nav2 + safety. In the middle: a fleet adapter (the protocol translator from the robot's API to VDA 5050 or Open-RMF). At the top: the fleet manager (Open-RMF, Vecna, Otto Fleet) handles task assignment, traffic, charging. Plumbing: MQTT broker (Mosquitto, EMQX), monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), storage (PostgreSQL for tasks, time-series DB for telemetry).
bullets: Robot layer: ROS 2, Nav2, safety stack - the same as a single-robot deployment
Adapter layer: translates robot API ↔ fleet protocol (VDA 5050, RMF API)
Fleet layer: traffic supervisor + task dispatcher + charging scheduler
Integration layer: WMS / MES / ERP via REST + AMQP
Observability: every robot publishes /metrics for Prometheus scrape
Postmortem culture: every fleet downtime gets an incident review

codeBlocks.title: Open-RMF fleet adapter - minimum viable example
